prompt: Linda is 42 years old, lives with her adult daughter, and earned exactly $5,000 this year from part-time work. Her daughter provides more than half of Linda's total support. Does Linda qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Linda does not qualify. Under § 152(c), she fails the qualifying child (QC) age test — she is 42, far above the 18-year threshold. Under § 152(d), qualifying relative (QR) requires gross income LESS THAN the exemption amount (§ 152(d)(1)(B)). Linda earned exactly $5,000. Because the statute uses a strict inequality (< $5,000, not ≤), $5,000 does not satisfy this test. She therefore fails the QR gross income gate and cannot be claimed as a dependent.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Linda qualifies as a qualifying relative under § 152(d). Her daughter provides more than half her support, satisfying the support test. Her income of $5,000 meets the gross income limit, and she lives with her daughter year-round.

prompt: Sophie is 14 years old and has been placed with a foster family by a licensed state agency. The foster family provides all of her care and financial support. Sophie has no contact with her biological parents. Does Sophie qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Sophie qualifies as a qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). Under § 152(f)(1)(C), an eligible foster child placed by an authorized agency is treated as the taxpayer's child. Sophie satisfies all QC tests: (1) relationship — eligible foster child; (2) age — 14, under 19; (3) residency — lives with the foster family full-time; (4) self-support — the foster family provides all support. She qualifies as a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Sophie is not the foster family's biological or adopted child, so she does not satisfy the § 152(c)(2) relationship test. Foster placements do not create the legal parent-child relationship required by the tax code, and the foster family cannot claim her without formal adoption.

prompt: Carlos is 22 years old, is not enrolled in school, and lives with his parents. He works part-time earning $9,500 per year. His parents still cover rent and groceries. Does Carlos qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Carlos fails both dependency tests. Under § 152(c), qualifying child (QC): Carlos is 22 and not a full-time student, exceeding the 18-year age limit (or 23 for students). Under § 152(d), qualifying relative (QR): § 152(d)(1)(B) requires gross income strictly less than the exemption amount ($5,000). Carlos earns $9,500, far exceeding $5,000. He fails the QR gross income test and cannot be claimed as a dependent regardless of parental support.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Carlos qualifies as a qualifying relative under § 152(d) because his parents provide significant financial support and he lives in their household. The tax code allows dependent claims when parents cover more than half of total support, which applies here.

prompt: Maria is 25 years old and enrolled in a Ph.D. program. Her parents pay her tuition ($18,000/year) but Maria receives a graduate stipend of $22,000 that covers her living expenses. Does Maria qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Maria fails both tests. Under § 152(c), qualifying child (QC): Maria is 25, exceeding the maximum age of 23 even for full-time students (§ 152(c)(3)(A)(ii)). Under § 152(d), qualifying relative (QR): § 152(d)(1)(B) requires gross income less than $5,000. Maria's stipend of $22,000 is gross income far exceeding the threshold. Parental tuition payments do not override the age and income tests.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Maria qualifies because her parents pay for her education. The student exception in § 152(c) extends the age limit to 23 for full-time students, and graduate students who receive parental tuition support can be claimed as dependents.

prompt: Emma is 8 years old and lives full-time with her mother. Her mother provides all of her financial support. Emma has no income of her own. Does Emma qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Emma qualifies as a qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). She satisfies every required test: (1) relationship — she is her mother's child (§ 152(c)(2)); (2) residency — she lives with her mother for the full year, well over the required six months (§ 152(c)(1)(B)); (3) age — she is 8, well below the under-19 threshold (§ 152(c)(3)(A)(i)); (4) self-support — she provides none of her own support, far below the 50% ceiling (§ 152(c)(1)(D)); and (5) no joint return filed (§ 152(b)(2)). The citizenship test is presumed satisfied. Emma is therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Emma earns no income, which means she has not established herself as a financially independent individual. Dependents must demonstrate some level of financial participation. Since Emma contributes nothing financially, she fails to meet the requirements and cannot be claimed.

prompt: Tyler is 16 years old, lives with his father year-round, and has no earned income. His father covers all of his living expenses including housing, food, clothing, and medical care. Does Tyler qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Tyler qualifies as his father's qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). He meets all five tests: (1) relationship — child of the taxpayer (§ 152(c)(2)); (2) residency — lives with his father for the entire year (§ 152(c)(1)(B)); (3) age — 16 is under 19 (§ 152(c)(3)(A)(i)); (4) self-support — provides 0% of his own support (§ 152(c)(1)(D)); (5) no joint return (§ 152(b)(2)). Tyler is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Tyler qualifies, but only as a qualifying relative — not as a qualifying child. Because Tyler does not earn any income, he cannot meet the threshold to be a qualifying child. His father should claim him under the qualifying relative category instead.

prompt: Rachel is 22 years old and a full-time college student. She lives with her parents for the entire year and has no significant income. Her parents pay all her tuition, room expenses, and living costs. Does Rachel qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Rachel qualifies as a qualifying child (QC) under the student exception in § 152(c)(3)(A)(ii). Although she is over 18, a full-time student who is under 24 satisfies the age test. She also satisfies: (1) relationship — child of the taxpayers; (2) residency — lives with parents the entire year; (3) age — 22, under 24, full-time student; (4) self-support — provides essentially none; (5) no joint return. Rachel is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Rachel is 22 years old and an adult. The tax code only allows parents to claim dependents who are under 18. Once a child reaches adulthood, they must establish their own financial independence and cannot be claimed as a dependent regardless of student status.

prompt: Derek is 28 years old, is not a student, and has no disability. He lives with his parents and earned $2,500 from occasional freelance work this year. His parents pay for his rent, food, utilities, and all other major expenses, covering about 85% of his total support. Does Derek qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Derek does not qualify as a qualifying child (QC) because he is 28 — exceeding both the under-19 general limit and the under-24 student limit — and is not disabled. However, he qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1): (1) relationship — child of the taxpayers, satisfying § 152(d)(2)(A); (2) gross income — $2,500 is below the $5,000 exemption amount (§ 152(d)(1)(B)); (3) support — parents provide approximately 85%, well over the required 50%+ (§ 152(d)(1)(C)); (4) not a QC of any taxpayer (§ 152(d)(1)(D)). Derek qualifies as a dependent as a qualifying relative.
rejected: Answer: No. Derek is 28 years old and earns income, which means he is considered self-sufficient under the tax code. Adults who earn any income from employment cannot be claimed as dependents, regardless of how much their parents contribute to their expenses.

prompt: George is 72 years old and lives with his adult son David. George receives $3,000 per year in Social Security income. David provides approximately 80% of George's total financial support, including housing, food, and medical expenses. Does George qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. George qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1). He satisfies all four tests: (1) relationship — he is David's parent, an enumerated relationship under § 152(d)(2)(D); (2) gross income — $3,000 is below the $5,000 exemption amount (§ 152(d)(1)(B)); (3) support — David provides approximately 80%, exceeding the required 50%+ (§ 152(d)(1)(C)); (4) not a QC of any taxpayer — at 72, George cannot be anyone's QC (§ 152(d)(1)(D)). There is no upper age limit for qualifying relatives. George is a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. George is 72 years old. The tax code limits dependent claims to children and young relatives who are financially incapable of supporting themselves. Senior citizens who receive Social Security are considered self-supporting by the government and therefore cannot be claimed as dependents.

prompt: Margaret is 65 years old and lives with her daughter. Margaret receives $22,000 per year in pension income. Her daughter provides supplemental financial assistance, covering roughly 60% of Margaret's total support needs. Does Margaret qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Margaret cannot qualify as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1) because her gross income of $22,000 far exceeds the $5,000 exemption amount threshold required by § 152(d)(1)(B). The gross income test is a hard ceiling — if a potential QR's gross income equals or exceeds the applicable exemption amount, they are disqualified regardless of the support provided. Margaret's $22,000 pension income fails this test decisively.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Margaret qualifies because her daughter provides more than half of her total financial support. The tax code is designed to recognize family caregiving, and since the daughter covers 60% of Margaret's expenses, she should be able to claim her mother as a dependent.

prompt: Oliver is 12 years old. Following a family dispute, he has lived with his grandmother for only 4 months this year. His parents lived with him the other 8 months. The grandmother wishes to claim Oliver as a dependent. Does Oliver qualify as the grandmother's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Oliver does not qualify as his grandmother's qualifying child (QC) because the residency test requires the child to live with the taxpayer for more than half the year — more than 6 months (§ 152(c)(1)(B)). Oliver lived with his grandmother for only 4 months, which falls below that threshold. For the qualifying relative (QR) pathway, the relationship test is actually satisfied: Oliver is the grandmother's grandchild, a descendant of her child, which falls within § 152(d)(2)(A). However, QR status is categorically barred by § 152(d)(1)(D), which provides that an individual cannot be a qualifying relative of any taxpayer if that individual is a qualifying child of any taxpayer for the same year. Oliver lived with his parents for 8 months, satisfying their >6-month residency requirement, and his parents provide all of his financial support — making Oliver their qualifying child. Because Oliver is his parents' qualifying child, he cannot be the grandmother's qualifying relative. Oliver is not the grandmother's dependent.
rejected: Answer: Yes. The grandmother provided care and shelter for 4 months, which represents meaningful caregiving. The law recognizes the role of extended family members in providing support, and since the grandmother housed and cared for Oliver for a significant portion of the year, she should be entitled to claim him.

prompt: Janet is 80 years old and lives with her nephew Paul. Paul provides exactly 50% of Janet's financial support for the year. The remaining 50% comes from Janet's Social Security benefits. Janet's gross income this year is $4,200. Does Janet qualify as Paul's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Although Janet's gross income of $4,200 is below the $5,000 exemption amount and the relationship test is satisfied (aunt/nephew relationship falls under § 152(d)(2)(F), which covers a brother or sister of the taxpayer's father or mother), Janet fails the support test under § 152(d)(1)(C). The statute requires the taxpayer to furnish more than one-half of the individual's total support. Paul provides exactly 50% — this equals but does not exceed one-half. Because the statute demands strictly greater than 50%, Janet does not qualify as Paul's qualifying relative.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Paul provides half of Janet's total support, which meets the financial support threshold. The tax code requires that the taxpayer provide at least half of the dependent's support, and Paul satisfies that by covering exactly 50% of Janet's expenses.

prompt: Michael is 20 years old, a full-time college student living with his parents, and has no income. This year he married his longtime partner and the couple filed a joint federal tax return (not merely to claim a refund — they had an actual combined tax liability). His parents wish to claim him as a dependent. Does Michael qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Michael is otherwise a qualifying child — he is under 24, a full-time student, lives with his parents, and provides none of his own support. However, § 152(b)(2) bars any individual who files a joint return from being claimed as a dependent, unless the joint return is filed solely to claim a refund of withheld taxes or estimated tax payments and no tax liability would exist for either spouse filing separately. Because Michael and his spouse had an actual combined tax liability and filed a genuine joint return, the § 152(b)(2) exclusion applies. Michael cannot be claimed as a dependent.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Michael is a full-time student under 24 who lives with his parents and has no income. The fact that he is married does not affect his status as a dependent. Parents may claim married children as long as the children are students living in the family home.

prompt: Sarah is 30 years old, has been permanently disabled since a childhood accident, and lives with her sister year-round. Her sister provides 70% of Sarah's total financial support. Sarah earns $3,000 per year from a part-time job. Does Sarah qualify as a dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Sarah qualifies as her sister's qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). Normally the QC age limit is under 19 (or under 24 for students), but § 152(c)(3)(B) eliminates the age limit entirely for any individual who is permanently and totally disabled. Sarah's disability removes the age barrier. She satisfies: (1) relationship — sibling (§ 152(c)(2)(B)); (2) residency — lives with her sister the full year (§ 152(c)(1)(B)); (3) age — waived by disability; (4) self-support — $3,000 out of a total support pool in which the sister provides 70% means Sarah provides roughly 30%, well below 50% (§ 152(c)(1)(D)); (5) no joint return. Sarah is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Sarah is 30 years old, which exceeds the legal age limit for dependents. Although she has a disability, the tax code sets strict age boundaries for who can be claimed as a dependent. Adults over 24 years old, regardless of disability status, must meet different and stricter requirements that Sarah does not satisfy.

prompt: Tom's 8-year-old nephew Ben (son of Tom's deceased sister) lives with Tom full-time. Since the sister's passing, Tom has provided all of Ben's financial support. Does Ben qualify as Tom's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Ben qualifies as Tom's qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). The relationship test under § 152(c)(2)(C) includes descendants of a sibling, which encompasses a nephew. Ben satisfies: (1) relationship — son of Tom's sibling, i.e., descendant of sibling (§ 152(c)(2)(C)); (2) residency — lives with Tom the full year; (3) age — 8 years old, under 19; (4) self-support — Tom provides 100%, Ben provides 0%; (5) no joint return. Ben is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Ben is Tom's nephew, which is an indirect family relationship. Only direct relatives such as children, stepchildren, and siblings can be qualifying children. A nephew does not satisfy the relationship test for qualifying child status and would need to be evaluated under the more stringent qualifying relative rules instead.

prompt: Bob's mother-in-law Patricia lives with Bob and his wife in their home. Patricia earns $2,500 from part-time seasonal work. Bob and his wife together provide 80% of Patricia's total annual support. Does Patricia qualify as Bob's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Patricia qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1). The in-law relationship is expressly enumerated in § 152(d)(2)(G), which includes a father-in-law or mother-in-law. Patricia satisfies: (1) relationship — mother-in-law (§ 152(d)(2)(G)); (2) gross income — $2,500 is below the $5,000 exemption (§ 152(d)(1)(B)); (3) support — Bob and wife provide 80%, exceeding 50% (§ 152(d)(1)(C)); (4) not a QC of any taxpayer (§ 152(d)(1)(D)). Patricia is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. In-laws are not considered relatives for federal tax purposes. The dependent rules only cover blood relatives and legally adopted family members. A mother-in-law is not a blood relative of the taxpayer and therefore cannot be claimed as a dependent under any provision of § 152.

prompt: Two adult sisters, Diane (AGI $72,000) and Carol (AGI $55,000), share a home all year with their 8-year-old nephew Ethan, the son of their late brother. Both sisters are U.S. citizens and provide all of Ethan's financial support. Neither of their parents is alive to claim Ethan. Both sisters otherwise satisfy every qualifying child test. Which sister, if any, may claim Ethan as a qualifying child under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Diane (AGI $72,000) may claim Ethan. Both sisters satisfy the qualifying child tests: relationship (Ethan is the descendant of a sibling, § 152(c)(2)(C)), residency (12 months in the shared home), age (8, under 19), self-support (0%), and no joint return. Because two taxpayers each satisfy all QC tests, the § 152(c)(4) tie-breaker applies. No parent is available to claim Ethan, so § 152(c)(4)(B) awards the child to the claimant with the higher adjusted gross income. Diane's AGI of $72,000 exceeds Carol's $55,000, so Diane is the eligible claimant.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Carol may claim Ethan because she is the younger sister and therefore has a closer legal connection to the child. When two non-parent relatives compete to claim the same qualifying child, the tax code awards the child to the claimant who is most closely related by age. Since Carol is younger, she is the preferred claimant under § 152(c)(4).

prompt: Lisa is not related by blood or marriage to the taxpayer, but she has lived in the taxpayer's home for the entire calendar year. She earns $3,000 from occasional babysitting work. The taxpayer provides 75% of Lisa's total financial support throughout the year. Does Lisa qualify as the taxpayer's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Lisa can qualify as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1) through the household member provision. Section 152(d)(2)(H) includes any individual (other than the taxpayer's spouse) who has the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer for the entire taxable year. Lisa satisfies: (1) relationship — household member for the full year, not the taxpayer's spouse (§ 152(d)(2)(H)); (2) gross income — $3,000 is below the $5,000 exemption (§ 152(d)(1)(B)); (3) support — taxpayer provides 75%, exceeding 50% (§ 152(d)(1)(C)); (4) not a QC of any taxpayer (§ 152(d)(1)(D)). Lisa is a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Only blood relatives and legally recognized family members can be claimed as dependents. The tax code requires a formal family relationship — such as parent, child, sibling, or relative by marriage — between the taxpayer and the person they wish to claim. An unrelated individual who simply lives in the same home is not eligible.

prompt: Pedro is 8 years old and lives with his uncle in the United States. His uncle provides all of Pedro's financial support. Pedro is a citizen of Brazil, has no U.S. citizenship or permanent residency, and has never lived in Mexico or Canada. Does Pedro qualify as his uncle's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Pedro fails the citizenship and residency test under § 152(b)(3). To be claimed as a dependent, an individual must be a U.S. citizen or national, a U.S. resident alien, or a resident of a contiguous country (Canada or Mexico). Pedro is a Brazilian citizen with no U.S. status and no connection to Canada or Mexico. He is therefore categorically ineligible to be claimed as a dependent, regardless of the relationship or support provided.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Pedro lives in the United States with his uncle and receives full financial support. The dependency rules are based on physical presence, relationship, and support — all of which Pedro satisfies. The tax code does not impose citizenship requirements on children who physically reside in the U.S.

prompt: Four adult siblings each contribute 25% of their elderly mother Dorothy's annual support. Dorothy has $2,000 in interest income this year. The four siblings sign IRS Form 2120, a multiple support agreement, designating Sibling A as the one to claim Dorothy. Each of the other three siblings signs a written declaration agreeing not to claim Dorothy. Does Dorothy qualify as Sibling A's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Under the multiple support agreement provision of § 152(d)(3), when no single individual furnishes more than 50% of an individual's support but a group of eligible taxpayers collectively provides more than 50%, one member of the group who contributes more than 10% may claim the dependent — provided all other group members contributing more than 10% sign written declarations relinquishing their claim. Here: the four siblings together provide 100% (>50%); each provides 25% (>10%); the other three sign Form 2120. Dorothy's $2,000 income is below the $5,000 exemption. Sibling A may claim Dorothy as a qualifying relative.
rejected: Answer: No. Because no single sibling provides more than half of Dorothy's total support, no one can claim her as a dependent. The tax code requires the taxpayer to individually provide more than 50% of the dependent's support. Collective or shared support across multiple family members does not satisfy this requirement.

prompt: Daniel is 17 years old and lives with his parents. During the summer, he works full-time and earned $25,000, which he uses to pay approximately 60% of his own living expenses throughout the year. His parents pay the remaining 40%. Does Daniel qualify as his parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Daniel fails the self-support test for qualifying children under § 152(c)(1)(D). A qualifying child must not have provided more than half of their own support during the taxable year. Daniel covers 60% of his own living expenses, which exceeds the 50% threshold. Although he is under 19 and satisfies the age, relationship, and residency tests, the self-support test failure disqualifies him as a qualifying child. He also cannot be a qualifying relative because he could otherwise be a QC (§ 152(d)(1)(D) cross-reference to QC status). Daniel is not his parents' dependent.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Daniel is only 17 years old and lives with his parents, which automatically makes him eligible to be claimed as a dependent. The tax code recognizes that children under 18 are legally under their parents' care and authority, and income earned through part-time or summer work does not affect their dependent status.

prompt: Jennifer is 23 years old and enrolled full-time at a state university. During the school year, she lives in a campus dormitory. She returns to her parents' home for summer break and major holidays, spending approximately 3 months at home. Her parents pay all tuition, dormitory, and other expenses. Does Jennifer qualify as her parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Jennifer qualifies as a qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). The key issue is residency: although she is physically at the dorm for about 9 months, temporary absences for school are not counted against the taxpayer when determining whether the child 'lives with' the taxpayer under § 152(c)(1)(B). Jennifer's principal place of abode remains her parents' home; the dorm is a temporary absence. She satisfies: (1) relationship — child; (2) residency — treated as living with parents the full year; (3) age — 23, full-time student, under 24; (4) self-support — parents pay 100%; (5) no joint return. Jennifer is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Jennifer lives in a campus dormitory for approximately nine months out of the year, which means she does not satisfy the requirement of living with her parents for more than six months. The tax code counts only the months a child is physically present in the taxpayer's home. Three months of physical presence at home is insufficient.

prompt: Vincent is 45 years old, lives with his brother, and earned exactly $4,999 this year — just below the applicable $5,000 exemption amount. His brother provides 65% of Vincent's total annual support. Does Vincent qualify as his brother's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Vincent qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1). He satisfies all four tests: (1) relationship — brother, an enumerated relationship under § 152(d)(2)(C); (2) gross income — $4,999 is strictly less than the $5,000 exemption amount (§ 152(d)(1)(B)); (3) support — brother provides 65%, exceeding the required 50%+ (§ 152(d)(1)(C)); (4) not a QC of any taxpayer at age 45 (§ 152(d)(1)(D)). Vincent is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Vincent is 45 years old and earns income from employment. Adults of working age who have income are presumed to be self-supporting and do not qualify as dependents. The dependency rules are intended for children and individuals who are genuinely unable to work or earn income.

prompt: Divorced parents share custody of their 10-year-old daughter Emma. Emma lives with her mother for 7 months of the year and with her father for the remaining 5 months. Both parents claim Emma on their respective tax returns, arguing over who is entitled. The father's AGI is $90,000; the mother's AGI is $60,000. All other qualifying child factors (relationship, support, no joint return) are satisfied by both. Which parent, if any, may claim Emma as a qualifying child under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. The mother may claim Emma. The qualifying child residency test (§ 152(c)(1)(B)) requires the child to have the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer for more than half of the taxable year — strictly more than 6 months. The mother satisfies this test: Emma lives with her for 7 months. The father does not satisfy the residency test: Emma lives with him for only 5 months, which is less than the required threshold. Because the father cannot satisfy the basic QC residency requirement, he never becomes an eligible claimant and the § 152(c)(4) tie-breaker is not reached. The mother claims Emma by default as the only parent who qualifies.
rejected: Answer: Yes. The father may claim Emma because his AGI of $90,000 is higher than the mother's $60,000. Under the § 152(c)(4) tie-breaker, when both parents claim the same child, the parent with the higher adjusted gross income is entitled to claim the child. Since the father earns more, he wins the tie-breaker and is the eligible claimant.

prompt: Mark is a U.S. citizen who legally adopted a 5-year-old child from South Korea. The child is a South Korean citizen and has been living with Mark in the United States for the entire calendar year. Mark provides all of the child's financial support. Does the child qualify as Mark's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. The child qualifies as Mark's qualifying child (QC). Although the child is not a U.S. citizen or resident alien, § 152(b)(3)(B) provides an exception to the citizenship/residency test for adopted children: if the taxpayer is a U.S. citizen or national and the adopted child has the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer for the entire taxable year, the citizenship/residency requirement is waived. Mark is a U.S. citizen, the child lives with him all year, and all other QC tests (relationship as adopted child, age, residency, no self-support) are satisfied. The child is a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. The adopted child is not a U.S. citizen or legal resident, and the tax code requires all dependents to be U.S. citizens, nationals, or residents. International adoption does not automatically confer citizenship or tax-law residency status on the child, and therefore the child cannot be claimed as a dependent.

prompt: Carol's husband has a 14-year-old son, Ethan, from a previous marriage. Ethan lives with Carol and her husband full-time throughout the entire year. Carol's husband provides all of Ethan's financial support. Does Ethan qualify as Carol's husband's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Ethan qualifies as a qualifying child (QC) of Carol's husband. Section 152(f)(1)(B) expressly includes a stepchild within the definition of 'child' for purposes of § 152. Ethan satisfies: (1) relationship — stepchild (§ 152(f)(1)(B)); (2) residency — lives with his father and Carol full-time; (3) age — 14, under 19; (4) self-support — all support provided by father; (5) no joint return. Ethan is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Ethan is technically Carol's husband's son from a different relationship, not Carol's biological or adopted child. The tax code only allows a taxpayer to claim individuals who are their own biological children or legally adopted children. A stepchild from a prior relationship does not qualify under the dependent rules.

prompt: Robert has a half-sister Emma who is 10 years old. Emma lives with Robert full-time, and Robert provides all of her financial support since their shared parent passed away. Does Emma qualify as Robert's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Emma qualifies as Robert's qualifying child (QC). Section 152(f)(4) expressly states that the term 'brother or sister' includes half-siblings. Emma therefore satisfies the relationship test under § 152(c)(2)(B) as Robert's sibling. She also satisfies: (2) residency — lives with Robert the full year; (3) age — 10, under 19; (4) self-support — Robert provides 100%; (5) no joint return. Emma is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Emma and Robert share only one parent, making her a half-sibling — not a full sibling. The tax code uses precise biological relationship categories and does not include half-siblings within the sibling relationship test for qualifying children. A half-sibling must qualify, if at all, under the qualifying relative rules.

prompt: Stephanie is 26 years old, is not a student, and has no disability. She lives with her parents and earned $2,000 from occasional freelance work this year. Her parents provide approximately 90% of her total financial support. Does Stephanie qualify as her parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Stephanie does not qualify as a qualifying child (QC) because she is 26 — over both the under-19 limit and the under-24 student limit — and is not disabled. However, she qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR) under § 152(d)(1): (1) relationship — she is the taxpayers' daughter, satisfying § 152(d)(2)(A); (2) gross income — $2,000 is well below the $5,000 exemption amount; (3) support — parents provide approximately 90%, far exceeding 50%; (4) not a QC of any other taxpayer at age 26. Stephanie is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Stephanie is 26 years old and earns income. Once children reach adulthood and enter the workforce — even part-time — they lose eligibility as dependents. The tax code is designed to support dependent minors and students, not working adults who happen to reside with their parents.

prompt: Alan and Beth share custody of their son Connor (age 9). Connor qualifies as Beth's qualifying child because he lives with her for 8 months. Alan, who has Connor for only 4 months, wants to claim Connor as a qualifying relative under § 152(d) to obtain some tax benefit. Can Alan claim Connor as a qualifying relative? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Section 152(d)(1)(D) provides that a qualifying relative cannot be an individual who is a qualifying child of the taxpayer or of any other taxpayer for the taxable year. Because Connor is Beth's qualifying child for the year, Alan is categorically barred from claiming Connor as a qualifying relative. The statute explicitly forecloses the qualifying relative pathway when the individual is already someone else's qualifying child.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Alan can claim Connor as a qualifying relative because he provides financial support during the 4-month custody period and Connor is his biological child. The qualifying relative rules are independent of the qualifying child rules, and a parent always retains the option to claim their child under whichever dependency category best applies.

prompt: David is 35 years old, severely and permanently disabled, and lives with his parents. He earns $4,500 per year working at a sheltered workshop as part of his formal medical rehabilitation program. His parents provide 75% of his total financial support. The sheltered workshop income is incident to his medical care. Does David qualify as his parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. David qualifies as his parents' qualifying child (QC) under § 152(c). His disability eliminates the age requirement (§ 152(c)(3)(B)). Additionally, under § 152(d)(4), income earned from a sheltered workshop by a disabled individual, where the work is incident to the individual's medical care and the sheltered workshop is operating for that purpose, is excluded from gross income for QR purposes — though for QC self-support analysis, the income still matters. David's self-support percentage: if parents provide 75% of total support, David provides 25%, well below 50%. All QC tests are satisfied: (1) child relationship; (2) residency — full year; (3) age — waived by disability; (4) self-support — 25% ≤ 50%; (5) no joint return. David is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. David earns income from the sheltered workshop, which means he is employed and earning a wage. Working individuals, regardless of disability, are considered financially active and therefore ineligible to be claimed as dependents. The tax code requires dependents to be financially passive — i.e., not earning any income.

prompt: Ana is a Mexican national who holds a U.S. permanent resident card (green card). She lives with her adult daughter in the United States and earns $3,000 per year from a home craft business. Her daughter provides 80% of Ana's total annual support. Does Ana qualify as her daughter's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Ana satisfies the citizenship/residency test under § 152(b)(3) as a U.S. resident alien (lawful permanent resident). A green card holder is a U.S. resident for this purpose. She also qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR): (1) relationship — mother, an enumerated relationship under § 152(d)(2)(D); (2) gross income — $3,000 is below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — daughter provides 80%, exceeding 50%; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer at her age. Ana is a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Ana is not a U.S. citizen and therefore fails the citizenship requirement that the tax code imposes on all dependents. The law requires dependents to be American citizens, and foreign nationals — even those living in the United States with a green card — do not meet this standard.

prompt: Juan is a Mexican citizen who lives in Mexico, near the U.S. border. His U.S. citizen brother provides 70% of Juan's total annual financial support, including remittances for food, housing, and medical care. Juan earns $2,000 per year from a small business. Does Juan qualify as his brother's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Juan satisfies the citizenship/residency test under § 152(b)(3)(A)(iii), which extends eligibility to residents of a contiguous country — Mexico qualifies. He is therefore eligible to be claimed as a dependent. As a qualifying relative: (1) relationship — brother (§ 152(d)(2)(C)); (2) gross income — $2,000 is below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — brother provides 70%, exceeding 50%; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer. Juan is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Juan lives in Mexico and does not reside in the United States. The tax code only permits U.S. residents or citizens to be claimed as dependents. Individuals living outside the United States are subject to foreign tax laws and cannot be claimed on a U.S. federal income tax return.

prompt: Ashley is 19 years old, a full-time college student, and lives with her parents. She married this year and filed a joint federal tax return with her spouse — but the joint return was filed solely to claim a refund of federal income tax that had been withheld from Ashley's part-time job paychecks. Neither Ashley nor her spouse would have owed any tax if they had filed separately. Her parents wish to claim her. Does Ashley qualify as her parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Ashley qualifies as her parents' qualifying child (QC). The § 152(b)(2) joint return exclusion does not apply when the joint return is filed solely to obtain a refund of withheld taxes or estimated tax payments and neither spouse would have a tax liability if filing separately. Ashley's joint return falls squarely within this exception: it was filed only for a refund, and no independent tax liability exists for either spouse. All other QC tests are satisfied (student under 24, child relationship, full-year residency with parents, no self-support). Ashley is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Ashley filed a joint tax return with her spouse, which automatically and unconditionally disqualifies her from being claimed as a dependent. The tax code treats any married individual who files jointly as independent for dependency purposes. There is no exception to this rule.

prompt: Chris is 18 years old and 364 days old as of December 31 of the tax year (he turns 19 the next day). He lives with his parents the full year and has no income. His parents provide all of his financial support. Does Chris qualify as his parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Chris qualifies as his parents' qualifying child (QC). The age test under § 152(c)(3)(A)(i) requires the individual to be under age 19 at the close of the calendar year. Chris is 18 years old on December 31 — he has not yet turned 19 — so he satisfies the under-19 requirement. All other QC tests are also met: relationship (child), residency (full year), self-support (none). Chris is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Chris is about to turn 19, which means he is transitioning out of the eligible age range for dependents. The tax code applies age cutoffs prospectively based on the child's trajectory toward adulthood. Since Chris will be 19 very shortly, he should be treated as an adult for tax purposes for the current year.

prompt: Beth's stepmother Nancy (who married Beth's father and lived with them for many years; Beth's father passed away two years ago) now lives with Beth. Nancy earns $3,500 per year from a small pension. Beth provides 60% of Nancy's total annual support. Does Nancy qualify as Beth's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Nancy qualifies as Beth's qualifying relative (QR). Section 152(d)(2)(F) expressly lists 'stepfather or stepmother' as an eligible relationship for qualifying relatives. Importantly, the stepparent relationship does not dissolve upon the biological parent's death for purposes of § 152. Nancy satisfies: (1) relationship — stepmother (§ 152(d)(2)(F)); (2) gross income — $3,500 is below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — Beth provides 60%, exceeding 50%; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer. Nancy is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. The stepparent relationship existed through Beth's father, who is now deceased. Once the connecting biological parent passes away, the legal and tax-law relationship between the surviving stepparent and the stepchild ceases to exist. Nancy is no longer legally related to Beth in any way the tax code recognizes.

prompt: A taxpayer's uncle wants to claim his 10-year-old nephew (his brother's son) as a qualifying child. The child's mother (the legal parent, the taxpayer's sister-in-law) also files a return and claims the child. The child lived with his mother all year. The uncle provided substantial financial support. Can the uncle claim the nephew as a qualifying child or qualifying relative? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. The nephew is the mother's qualifying child for the taxable year — he lived with her all year, satisfies the age and relationship tests, and she claimed him. Section 152(d)(1)(D) bars the qualifying relative pathway when the individual is a qualifying child of any taxpayer for the taxable year. Additionally, the uncle loses the QC tie-breaker because the mother is the parent and the uncle is not — § 152(c)(4)(A) gives priority to the parent over a non-parent. The uncle cannot claim the nephew under either pathway.
rejected: Answer: Yes. The uncle can claim the nephew as a qualifying relative based on the substantial financial support he provided. The qualifying relative rules allow any individual who provides more than 50% of support to claim the person as a dependent, regardless of whether another taxpayer has also attempted to claim the same individual.

prompt: Michelle is exactly 24 years old on December 31 of the tax year and is enrolled full-time at law school. She lives with her parents the entire year and has no income whatsoever. Her parents pay all of her law school tuition and living expenses. Does Michelle qualify as her parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Michelle does not qualify as a qualifying child (QC). The student age exception under § 152(c)(3)(A)(ii) requires the individual to be under age 24 — meaning strictly less than 24 — at the close of the calendar year. Michelle is exactly 24 on December 31, so she fails the QC age test. However, she qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR): (1) relationship — daughter (§ 152(d)(2)(A)); (2) gross income — $0, well below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — parents provide 100%; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer (she is too old). Michelle is a qualifying relative and therefore still a dependent — just under a different category.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Michelle qualifies as a qualifying child. The law allows full-time students to be claimed as qualifying children until they turn 25. Since Michelle is enrolled in law school and only 24 years old, she still falls within the student exception and is her parents' qualifying child.

prompt: Ted's niece Patricia is 35 years old, permanently and totally disabled, and lives with Ted full-time. Patricia earns $1,000 per year from a small craft sale. Ted provides 80% of Patricia's total financial support. Does Patricia qualify as Ted's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Patricia qualifies as Ted's qualifying child (QC). Although she is 35, her permanent and total disability eliminates the age requirement under § 152(c)(3)(B). Patricia is Ted's niece — which means she is a descendant of Ted's sibling, satisfying the relationship test under § 152(c)(2)(C). She satisfies: (1) relationship — descendant of sibling (niece) (§ 152(c)(2)(C)); (2) residency — full year with Ted; (3) age — waived by disability; (4) self-support — $1,000 out of Ted's 80% means Patricia contributes roughly 20%, below 50%; (5) no joint return. Patricia is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Patricia is a niece, which is only an eligible relationship under the qualifying relative rules, not the qualifying child rules. Qualifying children must be the taxpayer's own child, stepchild, or sibling — a niece or nephew is always a qualifying relative at best, and Patricia's age and income likely disqualify her even under those rules.

prompt: James is 20 years old and a full-time student at a university located 300 miles from his parents' home. He lives in a campus apartment during the academic year (about 9 months) and returns to his parents' home during summer and winter breaks (about 3 months). His parents pay all tuition, rent, and living expenses. Does James qualify as his parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. James qualifies as his parents' qualifying child (QC). The temporary absence rule applicable to qualifying children treats absences due to school attendance as if the child continued to reside with the taxpayer. James's campus apartment is a temporary absence for educational purposes, not a change of his principal place of abode. His tax home remains with his parents. He satisfies: (1) relationship — son; (2) residency — treated as full year with parents per the temporary absence rule; (3) age — 20, full-time student, under 24; (4) self-support — parents pay 100%; (5) no joint return. James is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. James lives at his campus apartment for nine months of the year, meaning his primary residence is his college apartment, not his parents' home. The tax code counts actual physical presence: since James is physically present at his parents' home for only three months, he does not satisfy the more-than-six-months residency test.

prompt: Helen is 60 years old and lives with her son. She earns exactly $5,000 this year — equal to the applicable exemption amount of $5,000. Her son provides 80% of her total financial support. Does Helen qualify as her son's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Helen fails the gross income test for qualifying relatives under § 152(d)(1)(B). The statute requires that the individual's gross income be less than the exemption amount — strictly less than, not equal to. Helen earns exactly $5,000, which is equal to but not less than the applicable $5,000 exemption amount. She therefore fails this test, even though the son provides 80% of her support and the relationship test (mother) is satisfied. The strict 'less than' standard is not met.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Helen earns $5,000, which is exactly at the exemption threshold. When income equals the exemption amount, the taxpayer is at the boundary of eligibility, and the tax code treats boundary cases as satisfying the income requirement. Since Helen is not over the limit, she qualifies as a dependent.

prompt: Robert is 40 years old and lives with his sister. He earns $8,000 this year. His sister provides $6,000 toward his total annual living expenses of $14,000, while Robert covers the remaining $8,000 himself. Does Robert qualify as his sister's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Robert fails on two grounds. First, his gross income of $8,000 exceeds the $5,000 exemption amount, failing the § 152(d)(1)(B) gross income test. Second, even if income were not an issue, his sister provides only $6,000 of $14,000 total support — approximately 43% — which falls short of the required more-than-50% threshold under § 152(d)(1)(C). The taxpayer's own funds (the $8,000 Robert earns and spends on himself) count as part of his total support, reducing the sister's percentage. Robert is not a dependent.
rejected: Answer: Yes. The sister provides $6,000 toward Robert's expenses, which represents a major financial contribution. The tax code rewards taxpayers who provide meaningful financial assistance to family members in need. Since the sister is clearly helping support Robert significantly, she should be entitled to claim him as a dependent.

prompt: Tim is 22 years old, a full-time student, and lives with his parents. He recently married but he and his wife file separate federal tax returns (not a joint return). His parents provide all of his financial support and wish to claim him. Does Tim qualify as his parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Tim qualifies as his parents' qualifying child (QC). The § 152(b)(2) joint return exclusion applies only to individuals who file a joint return. Tim and his spouse file separate returns, so the exclusion does not apply. Tim satisfies: (1) relationship — son (child); (2) residency — lives with parents the full year; (3) age — 22, full-time student, under 24; (4) self-support — parents provide 100%; (5) no joint return — files separately. Tim is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Tim is married, and the tax code treats married individuals as financially independent adults who have formed their own household. Once a person marries, they can no longer be claimed as a dependent by their parents, regardless of whether they are students or whether their parents continue to provide financial support.

prompt: Lucy is 16 years old and lives with her parents. Due to a serious illness, she spent 7 months this year at an inpatient rehabilitation and treatment facility. Her parents pay all of her medical and living expenses and maintained her room at home throughout her absence. Does Lucy qualify as her parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Lucy qualifies as her parents' qualifying child (QC). The temporary absence rule provides that a child is treated as living with the taxpayer during periods of temporary absence due to special circumstances including illness and medical treatment. Lucy's 7-month stay at the rehabilitation facility is a temporary absence for medical reasons, not a change of her permanent residence. She remains a member of the parents' household. All QC tests are satisfied: relationship (child), age (16, under 19), self-support (parents pay all), no joint return. Lucy is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Lucy was physically located outside her parents' home for 7 months. The residency test is based on actual physical presence, not on where a parent maintains a room or wishes the child to be living. Seven months at a facility means Lucy does not satisfy the more-than-six-months residency requirement for the parents' home.

prompt: Three siblings each contribute 30% of their elderly father William's annual support, totaling 90%. None has signed any written agreement. The youngest sibling wants to claim William as a dependent. William has $1,500 in interest income. Does William qualify as the youngest sibling's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Although the three siblings collectively provide 90% of William's support and each provides more than 10%, the multiple support agreement provision under § 152(d)(3) requires all contributing group members (those providing more than 10%) to execute written declarations relinquishing their claim. Without the signed written agreements (IRS Form 2120), the multiple support exception does not apply. No individual sibling provides more than 50% on their own, so the basic QR support test also fails. William cannot be claimed.
rejected: Answer: Yes. The siblings together provide 90% of William's support, which clearly exceeds the 50% threshold. When family members collectively cover the majority of someone's financial needs, the tax code recognizes that this is a legitimate family support arrangement. Any one of the contributing siblings can claim William as a dependent.

prompt: Carlos Jr. is 10 years old. He was born in Mexico but immigrated to the U.S. with his father and holds a lawful permanent resident (green card) status. He has lived with his father in the United States the entire year. The father provides all financial support. Does Carlos Jr. qualify as his father's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Carlos Jr. satisfies the citizenship/residency test under § 152(b)(3). A lawful permanent resident (green card holder) qualifies as a U.S. resident for this purpose. As a 10-year-old child living with his father all year with no income and full parental support, he easily satisfies all qualifying child tests: relationship (child), residency (full year), age (under 19), self-support (zero), no joint return. Carlos Jr. is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Carlos Jr. was born in Mexico and is not a U.S. citizen. The tax code requires dependents to be U.S. citizens or nationals. A green card gives an individual the right to work and reside in the U.S., but it does not confer citizenship status, which is what the dependency rules require.

prompt: Frank is 25 years old and lives with his older sister Helen (age 32). Frank is not a student, not disabled, and earns $1,800 from occasional work. Helen provides approximately 80% of Frank's total annual support. Does Frank qualify as Helen's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Frank does not qualify as a qualifying child (QC) because he is 25 and neither a student nor disabled, exceeding the age limits. However, he qualifies as Helen's qualifying relative (QR): (1) relationship — brother, enumerated under § 152(d)(2)(C); (2) gross income — $1,800 is well below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — Helen provides 80%, exceeding the 50% threshold; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer at age 25. Frank is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Frank is an adult who is capable of working and earns income. The dependency rules were designed for children and elderly individuals who are completely unable to support themselves. A 25-year-old who earns any employment income is legally and financially independent and cannot be claimed as a dependent by a sibling.

prompt: Sam is 22 years old and tries to claim his 30-year-old brother Tom as a qualifying child. Tom lives with Sam, has no income, and Sam provides all of Tom's financial support. Tom is not a student and not disabled. Can Sam claim Tom as a qualifying child under 26 U.S.C. § 152(c)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: No. Sam cannot claim Tom as a qualifying child. Among the qualifying child requirements is that the child must be younger than the taxpayer (§ 152(c)(3)(A)). Tom is 30 years old while Sam is only 22 — Tom is older than Sam. This age comparison requirement independently disqualifies Tom from being Sam's qualifying child, regardless of the living arrangement or support facts. Sam may, however, explore the qualifying relative pathway if Tom meets those tests.
rejected: Answer: Yes. Tom is Sam's brother and lives in the same household. The qualifying child rules focus on relationship, residency, and support. Since Sam and Tom are brothers living together and Sam provides all financial support, the relationship and support tests are satisfied. The tax code does not require the qualifying child to be younger than the taxpayer.

prompt: A grandson wishes to claim his grandmother Rose as a dependent. Rose is 75 years old, lives with her grandson, and receives $4,000 per year in Social Security income. The grandson provides 70% of Rose's total financial support. Does Rose qualify as the grandson's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Rose qualifies as a qualifying relative (QR). Section 152(d)(2)(E) includes 'an ancestor of the taxpayer' as an eligible relationship, and a grandmother is an ancestor. Rose satisfies: (1) relationship — grandmother (ancestor) (§ 152(d)(2)(E)); (2) gross income — $4,000 is below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — grandson provides 70%, exceeding 50%; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer at age 75. Rose is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Grandparents fall outside the recognized dependency relationships. The tax code's dependent rules only recognize parents, children, and siblings as eligible relatives. Grandparents and grandchildren exist one generation removed and are not eligible for the qualifying relative relationship test.

prompt: Alex and his domestic partner Chris are not married and are not related by blood or marriage. Chris has lived with Alex for the entire calendar year. Chris earns $3,000 from part-time work. Alex provides 70% of Chris's total annual support. Does Chris qualify as Alex's dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Chris can qualify as a qualifying relative (QR) through the household member provision. Section 152(d)(2)(H) includes any individual who has the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer for the entire taxable year and is not the taxpayer's spouse. Chris has lived with Alex all year, is not Alex's spouse, and was not a member of Alex's household only temporarily. He satisfies: (1) relationship — household member for the full year, non-spouse (§ 152(d)(2)(H)); (2) gross income — $3,000 below the $5,000 exemption; (3) support — Alex provides 70%, exceeding 50%; (4) not a QC of any taxpayer. Chris is a qualifying relative and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Domestic partners are not recognized as dependents under the federal tax code. Dependency relationships require a formal legal or biological family connection. An unmarried partner who simply shares a home is not a family member in any legally recognized sense and therefore cannot be claimed as a dependent.

prompt: Patricia is 40 years old and has been permanently and totally disabled since birth. She lives with her parents and receives $2,000 per year in disability benefit payments. Her parents provide 70% of her total annual support. Does Patricia qualify as her parents' dependent under 26 U.S.C. § 152(a)-(d)? Begin your response with exactly 'Answer: Yes' or 'Answer: No', then explain your reasoning.
chosen: Answer: Yes. Patricia qualifies as her parents' qualifying child (QC). The QC age limit — normally under 19 or under 24 for students — is entirely eliminated for any individual who is permanently and totally disabled at any time during the year (§ 152(c)(3)(B)). Patricia satisfies: (1) relationship — daughter (child); (2) residency — lives with parents the full year; (3) age — disability waives the age requirement; (4) self-support — $2,000 out of a total support pool where parents provide 70% means Patricia provides approximately 30%, below the 50% ceiling; (5) no joint return. Patricia is a qualifying child and therefore a dependent.
rejected: Answer: No. Patricia is 40 years old. Even with a permanent disability, adults over the age of 24 cannot be claimed as qualifying children. The disability exception only applies to certain age-related tests and does not permit parents to claim adult children of any age. Patricia would need to qualify under the qualifying relative rules, which have separate income requirements.
